_meta.note: Pytania modułu M4. Metadane z master allocation (rozkład globalny). Plik <800 LOC (AGENTS).

questions.learningOutcome: The participant explains what an embedding is as a numerical representation of the meaning of text.
questions.prompt: An embedding model converts a phrase from QA documentation into an embedding. What does the resulting vector of numbers actually represent?

options.text: The semantic meaning of the text in numerical form — phrases with similar meaning produce similar vectors.
The number of words in the phrase and its length in characters.
The exact record of consecutive letters encoded as ASCII codes.
A numerical score for the grammatical and stylistic correctness of the text.

questions.feedbackCorrect: An embedding encodes the meaning of text as a vector of numbers, so that phrases close in meaning (e.g. „login” and „authentication”) end up close to each other.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: An embedding does not count characters, letters or grammar — it encodes meaning. Go back to the „A word as a vector” screen.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant interprets cosine similarity as a measure of the direction of vectors, independent of text length.
questions.prompt: In the NotkaApp corpus you have a short description „Password reset: e-mail link valid for 30 min” and a long, elaborate paragraph describing the same reset procedure. Why is their cosine similarity high despite the large difference in text length?

options.text: Cosine similarity measures the angle (direction) between meaning vectors, not their length — both texts point in the same direction.
Cosine similarity grows with the number of words, so a longer text always has higher similarity.
Cosine similarity compares the number of shared words, and both texts contain the word „reset”.
Cosine similarity always returns 1.0 when the texts concern the same product.

questions.feedbackCorrect: Cosine similarity computes the angle between vectors, i.e. the agreement in the direction of meaning — the length/„weight” of the text does not affect the result, so a short and a long description of the same thing are close.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: It is neither the number of words nor the shared tokens that decides, but the direction of the meaning vector. Review „Cosine similarity — direction, not length”.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant correctly interprets a ranking of nearest neighbors and understands the conventional nature of the similarity threshold.
questions.prompt: A semantic search returned a list of nearest neighbors with the results: fragment A = 0.91, fragment B = 0.74, fragment C = 0.62. How should a tester interpret this?

options.text: The list is sorted in descending order of similarity, but a high position does not guarantee business relevance — the content must be verified.
Fragment A with a score of 0.91 is certainly the correct answer, because it exceeds the 0.9 threshold.
Results below 0.75 are always wrong and can be rejected without checking.
A score of 0.62 means that fragment C is exactly 62% correct on the merits.

questions.feedbackCorrect: The ranking orders results in descending order, but the threshold is conventional and corpus-dependent — a high position is a signal, not proof of relevance.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: There is no fixed, universal threshold, and a numerical score is not a „percentage of correctness”. Go back to „How to read a similarity ranking”.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant selects the characteristics of a good query for semantic search.
questions.prompt: You want to find in the NotkaApp documentation information about the validity period of the password reset link in the current version. Which practices for formulating a semantic query will increase the relevance of the results? Select all correct answers.

options.text: Use a full phrase that captures the intent, e.g. „validity period of the password reset link”, instead of a single word.
Specify the context and version, e.g. „password reset in NotkaApp v2”, if we care about the current wording.
Formulate the query as a negation alone, e.g. „not session token”, in order to filter out other results.
Type a single general word „password”, hoping the model will figure out the intent on its own.
Include the key object of the query (the reset link), in order to move away from similar but different concepts (the session token).

questions.feedbackCorrect: A full phrase capturing the intent, specifying the context/version, and indicating the key object steer the query vector toward the right meaning.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: A single word is too general, and a negation alone works poorly in semantic search (the model still encodes the topic „session token”). Review „Choosing a query for semantic search”.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes situations in which high similarity does not mean a correct answer.
questions.prompt: In which of the following situations can a high cosine similarity score be misleading, returning a fragment that is semantically close but business-wise incorrect? Select all correct answers.

options.text: The fragment „login does not work” comes up as close for the query „login works” (negation/antonym).
A description of the payments feature comes up as close for a bug report about the same feature (same topic, different intent).
A fragment about a security „token” comes up for a query about a „token” in the sense of an LLM unit (ambiguity/polysemy).
An old wording of a requirement is very similar to the new one, although only the new one is correct (outdated version).
Two fragments describing exactly the same, current password reset procedure produce high similarity.

questions.feedbackCorrect: These are four classic similarity traps: negation, the same topic with a different intent, polysemy, and an outdated version — an embedding encodes the topic more strongly than small, critical differences.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Option E is the case where high similarity is RELEVANT (two consistent, current descriptions of the same thing). Review „Trap: similar ≠ correct — 4 cases”.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant evaluates why embeddings confuse critical differences and draws the right conclusion for QA work.
questions.prompt: Why does an embedding model confuse pairs such as „login works” / „login does not work”, and what conclusion should a tester draw from this? Select all the accurate statements.

options.text: An embedding encodes the topic and style of a phrase more strongly than small but critical differences, such as a negation or a version number.
A similarity ranking is a supporting signal, not a verdict — the result requires verification of the fragment's content.
It is worth supporting the search with filtering by metadata (e.g. version, document type) to limit misleading hits.
Since the model confuses these pairs, semantic search should be abandoned entirely in QA.
It is enough to raise the similarity threshold to 0.95 to automatically eliminate all traps.

questions.feedbackCorrect: An embedding encodes the topic more strongly than a negation or a version, which is why we treat the ranking as a signal requiring content verification, supported by metadata filtering.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: Abandoning search (D) and a rigid threshold (E) are false solutions — a pair with a negation can be so similar that no threshold will filter it out. Review „Going deeper: why the model gets it wrong”.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant diagnoses, on a concrete case, why high similarity returned a business-wise incorrect fragment.
questions.prompt: In the NotkaApp corpus you enter the query „How long until the password reset link expires?”. As the closest match the search returns DOC1: „Login procedure: the session token expires after 30 min of inactivity” (0.86), and only lower down DOC3: „Password reset: e-mail link valid for 30 min, single-use” (0.71). What best explains this result?

options.text: DOC1 is semantically close because it shares with the query the theme „something expires after 30 min”, but it concerns a different object (the session token), so the correct source is DOC3 — this is the „similar but different concept” trap (same theme, different object).
DOC1 is the correct answer, because it has a higher similarity score than DOC3.
The result is an error of the embedding model, which should be reported as a library defect.
DOC3 is incorrect, because its score of 0.71 is below the universal relevance threshold of 0.75.

questions.feedbackCorrect: The shared theme „expires after 30 min” brings DOC1 close to the query, even though it concerns the session token and not the reset link — the correct source is DOC3, despite its lower score.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The highest score does not determine relevance, and 0.75 is not any universal threshold — this is the classic „similar but different concept” trap. Review the „Trap: similar ≠ correct” and „Semantic map” screens.

questions.learningOutcome: The participant recognizes the negation trap in search results and chooses a safe QA decision.
questions.prompt: On the „Semantic map” you enter the query „login does not work after the update”. The closest fragment (0.89) turns out to be „login works correctly”, while the fragment „known bug: login locks up after update v2” is only third (0.77). How best to assess this situation and react?

options.text: This is the negation trap: the embedding encodes the shared topic „login”, but loses „does not work” vs „works” — you should verify the content and choose the fragment about the known bug, despite its lower score.
Accept the fragment „login works correctly”, because it has the highest similarity 0.89, and close the analysis.
Consider both fragments equivalent, since both concern login, and decide nothing further.
Lower the threshold to 0.5 so that the ranking automatically moves the right fragment to the first position.

questions.feedbackCorrect: „Works” and „does not work” are semantically close because they differ only by the negation — the highest score is misleading here, so we verify the content and choose the fragment about the known bug.
questions.feedbackIncorrect: The highest score is the negation trap, and manipulating the threshold does not fix the direction of the vector — you have to check the content. Review „Trap: similar ≠ correct” and „Semantic map”.
